title: Why Am I Not Losing Weight in Malta? 8 Honest Reasons (and What Actually Helps)
excerpt: Francesca had been eating 1,400 calories a day for four months. Every meal weighed and logged. Every walk tracked. She stood on the scale each Monday morning in her bathroom in Birkirkara, and every Monday morning the number stayed exactly where it…
body_text: Last reviewed by the Carisma Medical Team — 2026 Francesca had been eating 1,400 calories a day for four months. Every meal weighed and logged. Every walk tracked. She stood on the scale each Monday morning in her bathroom in Birkirkara, and every Monday morning the number stayed exactly where it was. She had started to wonder — quietly, late at night when the kids were finally asleep — whether something was genuinely wrong with her. Whether she was one of those people for whom weight loss was simply impossible. She felt ashamed of even thinking that, as if it were an excuse. As if she was the problem. She was not the problem. A medical assessment at Carisma Slimming revealed insulin resistance that had gone undetected for years. Her body was not being resistant to change because she lacked commitment. It was chemically, biologically working against the very efforts she was making. Once that was identified and addressed, everything shifted. And Francesca is not unusual — not even slightly. If you are asking why am I not losing weight in Malta despite genuinely trying, the answer is almost never what you fear it is. It is rarely willpower. It is rarely effort. In our experience working with women across Malta, it is almost always one of eight specific, identifiable, treatable reasons. Here they are — honestly, without judgement. Key Takeaways - Hormonal imbalances like insulin resistance and thyroid dysfunction can block weight loss entirely - Poor sleep and chronic stress directly promote fat storage through ghrelin and cortisol - Several common medications have documented weight gain as a side effect - PCOS and perimenopause make standard diets structurally inadequate for many women - Biological plateaus are predictable and require recalibration, not more restriction - A clinically supervised medical assessment can uncover what diets never will When Trying Hard Is Not the Problem The diet industry has spent decades convincing us that weight is a maths problem. Eat less, move more. If it is not working, you must be doing one of those two things wrong. That framework is not only reductive — it is, for a significant number of women, simply medically incorrect. The human body is not a calculator. It is a dynamic, adaptive, hormonally complex system that responds to sleep, stress, reproductive cycles, medications, gut function, thyroid output, and dozens of other variables that have nothing to do with the number of calories on a nutrition label. When clinically supervised programmes investigate women who feel they are "failing" at weight loss, they routinely find underlying factors — hormonal imbalances, metabolic adaptation, medication interactions — that were invisible to every diet plan those women had tried. The diets were not wrong because the women were lazy. The diets were wrong because they were treating a medical problem like a lifestyle choice. Results may vary for each individual, and what is true for Francesca will not be true for every person reading this. But the principle holds: if you have been genuinely trying and genuinely not losing weight, there is usually a reason. And that reason deserves a proper investigation, not another spreadsheet. Reason 1: Your Hormones May Be Working Against You Insulin resistance and weight loss Insulin is the hormone that tells your cells to absorb glucose from the blood for energy. When cells become resistant to that signal — a condition called insulin resistance — the body compensates by producing more insulin. Elevated insulin levels actively promote fat storage, particularly around the abdomen, and make it harder for the body to access stored fat for fuel. Insulin resistance is also closely connected to glucagon-like peptide signalling, the mechanism that governs appetite suppression and blood glucose regulation — meaning the body's satiety system can become muted at the same time. Insulin resistance is extremely common in women in their 30s and 40s, particularly those with excess weight around the middle, poor sleep, or a history of gestational diabetes. It often has no obvious symptoms. It can coexist with normal fasting blood sugar, meaning a standard GP check may not catch it without specific testing. In a state of insulin resistance, even a carefully maintained calorie deficit can produce little or no scale movement — not because the deficit is not there, but because the hormonal environment is not allowing fat mobilisation to function as it should. This is one of the first things our medical team assesses at Carisma Slimming. Thyroid issues and slow metabolism The thyroid gland regulates the speed of your metabolic rate. An underactive thyroid (hypothyroidism) reduces how many calories your body burns at rest, causes fatigue, and makes weight loss significantly more difficult even with full dietary adherence. According to the NHS, hypothyroidism affects approximately 2% of the population but is more common in women, and symptoms — fatigue, feeling cold, weight gain, sluggishness — are often attributed to lifestyle rather than investigated medically. A simple blood test measuring TSH (thyroid stimulating hormone) can identify this within days. Left unidentified, it can make sustained weight loss all but impossible without treatment. Reason 2: Sleep Deprivation Drives Weight Gain How poor sleep affects ghrelin and leptin This one affects almost every busy mother, shift worker, and anxious woman in Malta: not sleeping enough is not just making you tired. It is biologically increasing your appetite and reducing your body's ability to feel full. Two hormones govern hunger and satiety. Ghrelin stimulates appetite — it rises when you are sleep-deprived. Leptin signals fullness — it falls when you are sleep-deprived. Research published in PLOS Medicine found that sleeping fewer than six hours per night was associated with significantly higher ghrelin levels and lower leptin levels, creating a hormonal environment that drives overeating even when conscious food choices remain disciplined. Sleep deprivation also raises cortisol levels (see Reason 3), impairs glucose regulation, and reduces the energy you have for any form of movement. It is not a lifestyle inconvenience — it is a medical barrier to weight loss. In a clinically supervised programme, sleep is assessed as a genuine clinical variable — not as a soft wellness checkbox. Reason 3: Chronic Stress and Cortisol Why cortisol promotes fat storage around the middle Cortisol is your primary stress hormone. Released by the adrenal glands during periods of perceived threat or pressure, it was designed to help your body respond to short-term emergencies — flooding the body with glucose for quick energy, suppressing non-essential functions, and storing energy reserves for future need. The problem is that modern life — the school run, the work deadlines, the family demands, the financial pressure — triggers that same cortisol response chronically, day after day. And chronically elevated cortisol has a very specific effect on the body: it promotes fat storage, particularly visceral (abdominal) fat. It also increases cravings for high-calorie, high-fat foods as the body tries to rebuild its "emergency reserves." Research published in the journal Obesity Reviews confirms that chronic psychological stress is independently associated with weight gain and obesity, regardless of dietary intake. If stress is a constant feature of your life, a calorie-controlled diet will always be fighting uphill. This is not a personality flaw. It is physiology. And in a clinically supervised programme, it can be addressed with the seriousness it deserves. Reason 4: You Are Eating Less Than You Think — But Not in the Right Way Hidden calories and liquid intake This is an uncomfortable truth, and it is worth saying gently: food tracking is genuinely difficult, and most people underestimate how much they eat — not because they are dishonest, but because human memory is poor and food labelling is imprecise. Studies consistently show that even dietitians, when asked to estimate their own calorie intake, underestimate by 10–15%. But there is a subtler version of this that catches many women off guard: liquid calories. A daily ftira coffee with milk, a glass of wine in the evening, a shop-bought smoothie at lunch — these can add 300–500 calories a day that feel like "nothing" because drinking is not registered by the brain as eating in the same way. Eating too little and slowing your metabolism Here is the other side of that same coin: eating far too little — below 1,200 calories per day consistently — can trigger a survival response in the body that reduces your metabolic rate. Research on adaptive thermogenesis (Rosenbaum M, Leibel RL, 2010, published in the New England Journal of Medicine) shows that the body can reduce its total daily energy expenditure by 200–400 calories beyond what weight loss alone would predict, essentially resisting continued deficit. The body reads extreme restriction as famine and protects itself accordingly. More is not always more when it comes to calorie cutting. A clinically supervised programme calibrates your intake precisely — not too high, not low enough to trigger metabolic slowdown. Reason 5: PCOS, Perimenopause, and Hormonal Changes Why standard diets fail for hormonal weight gain Two of the most common hormonal conditions affecting women in the weight loss conversation are polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) and perimenopause. Both fundamentally alter how the body processes food, stores fat, and responds to dietary changes. PCOS is associated with insulin resistance (see Reason 1), elevated androgens, and a hormonal environment that actively promotes weight gain — particularly around the abdomen — while making conventional calorie restriction less effective. Women with PCOS are also at higher risk of developing type 2 diabetes, which adds urgency to proper medical oversight. Perimenopause — the hormonal transition that typically begins in the mid-to-late 40s — involves declining oestrogen, which shifts fat distribution from the hips and thighs to the abdomen, reduces muscle mass, and reduces basal metabolic rate. Many women in Malta describe gaining weight "despite doing nothing differently" in their late 40s. They are right. Something changed — but it was hormonal, not behavioural. When medical assessment is essential Standard low-calorie diets are not designed for PCOS or perimenopausal physiology. They address calorie intake without addressing the hormonal root causes. This is why so many women with these conditions feel that "nothing works" — they are using the wrong tool. Results may vary for each individual, but a clinically supervised assessment that includes hormonal profiling, blood glucose testing, and a full history can identify these factors and create a programme that actually addresses them. Our team at Carisma Slimming in Malta includes the medical oversight to do exactly this. You can explore our full range of slimming packages to see how we approach each individual's circumstances with the right clinical tools. Reason 6: Medications That Cause Weight Gain This is one of the least-discussed reasons for weight loss resistance, and it affects more people than most realise. Several commonly prescribed medications have weight gain as a documented side effect. These include: Antidepressants — particularly SSRIs and tricyclics, which can increase appetite and reduce metabolic rate • Corticosteroids — such as prednisolone, used for inflammatory conditions • Beta-blockers — used for blood pressure and anxiety • Certain antihistamines — particularly older-generation antihistamines taken regularly • Some oral contraceptives and HRT formulations • Antipsychotic medications • Insulin and some diabetes medications — metformin is weight-neutral, but sulphonylureas and insulin can promote fat storage If you are taking any of these medications and struggling with your weight, this is not something to quietly accept. It is something to raise with your prescribing doctor. In a clinically supervised programme, your full medication list is reviewed as standard — because treating the whole person, not just the diet, is the only approach that works. Reason 7: You Have Hit a Biological Plateau Plateaus are not failures. They are one of the most predictable events in any weight loss journey — a built-in biological response that happens to almost everyone who loses weight. Clinically, a plateau is defined as four or more consecutive weeks with no meaningful scale change despite maintained adherence. The causes are real and documented: as body weight decreases, the body requires fewer calories to function (so the original deficit shrinks), leptin levels fall (driving increased hunger), and the body may reduce non-exercise energy expenditure to compensate for caloric restriction. Research on metabolic adaptation (Rosenbaum M, Leibel RL, 2010, NEJM) shows that adaptive thermogenesis can reduce total daily energy expenditure by 200–400 calories beyond what body composition changes alone would predict. In practical terms: the plan that worked in month one may no longer be sufficient in month four — through no fault of your own. Breaking a plateau requires recalibration, not punishment. Evidence-supported approaches include adjusting caloric targets to current body weight, increasing protein intake (research supports 1.6g per kilogram of body weight per day to preserve muscle mass), adding resistance training to maintain metabolic rate, and — in a clinically supervised programme — medication review or adjustment where appropriate. A plateau is not the end of the road. It is a signal that the approach needs updating. Our team at Carisma Slimming is experienced in identifying exactly what that update needs to be. Reason 8: You Need a Different Level of Support in Malta What a medical assessment can reveal that diets cannot If you have read through the seven reasons above and recognised yourself in more than one of them, there is a message in that: the solution you need is probably not another diet. It is a proper medical assessment. Here is what a thorough assessment at Carisma Slimming can uncover that no calorie-tracking app, no meal plan, and no group diet programme will find: Insulin resistance, even at pre-diabetic stages not yet caught on standard blood tests • Thyroid dysfunction — subclinical hypothyroidism in particular is frequently missed • Hormonal profiling (oestrogen, progesterone, testosterone, DHEA) relevant to weight gain patterns • Cortisol patterns consistent with chronic stress-driven fat retention • Medication interactions that are actively working against your progress • Metabolic rate measurement to identify adaptive thermogenesis A diet tells you to eat less. A clinically supervised programme tells you why the eating less is not working — and then addresses it. If you are ready to understand what is actually happening in your body, we would encourage you to book a free consultation — not as a commitment, but as an investigation. You deserve answers, not just another plan. You can also learn more about our full medically supervised weight loss programme in Malta to understand how we approach this comprehensively. FAQs: Why Am I Not Losing Weight in Malta? Can hormones really stop you from losing weight even if you eat well? Yes — and this is one of the most important things to understand. Hormones such as insulin, leptin, cortisol, thyroid hormones, oestrogen, and testosterone all directly regulate fat storage, fat mobilisation, appetite, and metabolic rate. When any of these are out of balance — due to conditions like insulin resistance, hypothyroidism, PCOS, or perimenopause — the body can actively work against a calorie deficit. Eating well is necessary but not always sufficient. A medical assessment can identify which hormones, if any, may be contributing to your difficulty losing weight. Is it possible to gain weight even on a calorie deficit? In the short term, yes — though it is usually temporary. Water retention, medication effects, hormonal fluctuations (particularly around the menstrual cycle or during perimenopause), and stress-driven cortisol can all cause the scale to rise or stay flat even when fat loss is occurring. It is also possible that the stated calorie deficit is smaller than believed, due to measurement inaccuracies or metabolic adaptation. If the scale has not moved in more than four weeks despite genuine adherence, a clinically supervised review of all contributing factors is the appropriate next step. Could my medication be causing my weight not to shift? Absolutely — and this is more common than most people realise. Antidepressants, corticosteroids, beta-blockers, some antihistamines, certain contraceptives, and a number of diabetes medications are all documented to promote weight gain or make weight loss significantly harder. This does not mean you should stop your medication — that decision is always between you and your prescribing doctor. But it does mean the medication picture should be part of any thorough weight loss assessment. In a clinically supervised programme, your full medication list is reviewed as a routine clinical step. Should I see a doctor if I cannot lose weight despite trying? Yes. If you have made genuine, sustained changes to your eating and activity for more than six to eight weeks and seen no meaningful change on the scale, this is worth investigating medically — not because something is necessarily seriously wrong, but because there are identifiable, treatable reasons why weight loss stalls. An NHS or similar standard GP visit is a good starting point for baseline blood tests. A clinically supervised slimming programme goes further — assessing hormones, metabolism, medication interactions, and lifestyle factors together, in the context of a weight loss plan. What tests can help explain unexplained weight gain? Useful tests include: fasting glucose and HbA1c (to identify insulin resistance and pre-diabetes), TSH and free T4 (thyroid function), a full hormonal panel (oestrogen, progesterone, testosterone, DHEA — especially relevant for women over 40), fasting insulin levels (standard glucose tests can miss insulin resistance), cortisol (where chronic stress is a factor), and a full blood count and iron studies (anaemia causes fatigue that reduces activity). In a clinically supervised programme, our clinical team will advise which of these are relevant to your specific situation. Results, and the plan built around them, may vary for each individual. Does perimenopause really make weight loss harder? Yes — measurably so, and for real biological reasons. Declining oestrogen levels during perimenopause shift fat distribution from the hips and thighs (subcutaneous) to the abdomen (visceral), reduce lean muscle mass, and lower basal metabolic rate. Research confirms that women in perimenopause often gain weight without any change in diet or activity — the hormonal shift alone changes body composition. Standard calorie-restricted diets do not address these hormonal drivers. A programme that accounts for perimenopausal physiology — with appropriate medical oversight — is structurally different from any diet you have tried before. You Are Not Failing. The Approach May Be. There is a version of you — not far from here — who gets out of bed in the morning without dreading the mirror. Who climbs the stairs at work without losing her breath at the top. Who carries the shopping in from the car in a single trip without her knees aching. Who sleeps well. Who has energy to actually be present in the evenings, instead of running on empty by 7pm. That version of you does not exist because she tried harder than you. She exists because she found out what was actually going on in her body — and got support that was equal to the complexity of the problem. You deserve support that fits your life, your biology, and your specific circumstances in Malta. Not a generic plan. Not a meal replacement shake. A proper, clinically supervised programme built around what is actually happening in your body. Book your free consultation at Carisma today. We will sit with you, listen to your history, and begin the process of understanding — not judging. That first conversation changes everything. Picture yourself a few weeks from now, not just lighter, but finally understanding your own body. With answers instead of confusion. With a plan that is actually built for you.
